questions.prompt: What is the through-line the chapter identifies as the mark of idiomatic senior Swift?

options.text: Letting the type system carry the weight — optionals for absence, value types for isolation, protocols for variation, throws for failure, actors for shared mutable state — each turning a class of runtime bug into something the compiler catches; anti-patterns are almost always ways of opting OUT of that checking.
Using as many advanced language features as possible in every file to demonstrate mastery.
Avoiding the type system in favor of runtime checks and dynamic dispatch for flexibility.
Writing everything as classes to maximize compatibility with Objective-C.

questions.explanation: The chapter's synthesis: idiomatic Swift lets the type system catch bug classes at compile time, and anti-patterns are ways of opting out (force-unwraps, Any, unannotated interop, reference types for data). (b) is feature-showmanship, not idiom. (c) inverts the thesis. (d) contradicts the struct-by-default guidance.

questions.prompt: Which of the following does the chapter name as anti-patterns that read as 'not really Swift'? Select all that apply.

options.text: Force-unwrap as a default to silence the compiler rather than model the possibility
A Massive View Controller cramming networking, state, and layout into one class
Using a reference type (class) for pure immutable data that a struct would model
Grouping a type's conformances into focused extensions to keep files navigable

questions.explanation: The chapter lists (a) force-unwrap-as-default, (b) Massive View Controller, and (c) reference types for pure data as anti-patterns — each an opt-out of the type system or of good structure. (d) is the opposite: using extensions to organize is an idiom the chapter endorses, not an anti-pattern.

questions.prompt: When you call an OLD, unannotated Objective-C API from Swift, what happens to null safety, and why?

options.text: The value imports as an implicitly-unwrapped optional — it looks non-optional but crashes if it's actually nil at the use site — because the unannotated header never states whether the pointer can be nil, so the compiler can't verify it; the guarantee is Swift-to-Swift and necessarily stops at a language that can't express nullability.
Swift refuses to compile any call to an unannotated Objective-C API until it is annotated.
Swift treats every unannotated value as a non-optional that can never be nil, fully safe.
Swift automatically wraps it in a proper optional and forces you to handle nil, so there is no risk.

questions.explanation: The chapter: unannotated Obj-C APIs import as implicitly-unwrapped optionals (look non-optional, crash on nil), because the header never stated nullability — the guarantee stops at the interop boundary, mirroring Kotlin/Java platform types. (b) is false; it compiles, it just imports as IUO. (c) is dangerously wrong — it can be nil and will crash. (d) describes annotated APIs, not unannotated ones.

questions.prompt: What is the cost of exposing Swift to Objective-C with @objc (or using #selector, KVO, older UIKit patterns)?

options.text: @objc forces Objective-C's dynamic message dispatch, giving up the static dispatch and inlining Swift normally uses — necessary for those interop features, but a performance and optimizer boundary worth being conscious of rather than sprinkling on for its own sake.
@objc has no runtime cost; it only changes how the symbol is named.
@objc makes the method faster by enabling aggressive inlining unavailable to pure Swift.
@objc converts the method to a value type, changing its copy semantics.

questions.explanation: The chapter: @objc forces dynamic message dispatch, forfeiting static dispatch/inlining — necessary for interop features but a real optimizer boundary. (b) understates it — there IS a dispatch cost, not just a naming change. (c) reverses it (dynamic dispatch prevents the inlining static dispatch allows). (d) fabricates a value-type conversion.

questions.prompt: Why does bridging Foundation types to Swift value types deserve attention on a hot path?

options.text: Foundation types bridge to Swift value types (e.g. the Objective-C string type to String, arrays to Array) often with a copy; convenient, but bridging a huge collection back and forth in a hot loop is a real cost a profiler will surface.
Bridging is always free because the types share identical memory layout.
Bridging permanently converts the type so it can never be used from Objective-C again.
Bridging only occurs at compile time and has no runtime representation.

questions.explanation: The chapter: bridging often involves a copy, convenient but costly for large collections in hot loops — a profiler-visible cost. (b) is false; a copy is frequently involved, so it's not free. (c) fabricates a one-way permanent conversion. (d) is false; bridging happens at runtime with real cost.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter's closing trade-off, why is 'idiom for its own sake' itself an anti-pattern?

options.text: The cleverest reduce, the most protocol-oriented design, or the most granular value types can each be LESS clear than a plain implementation; the staff-level skill is optimizing for the reader who maintains this in a year, which sometimes means the boring, obvious version wins.
Idiomatic code is always slower, so idioms should be avoided for performance.
Idioms are only for junior engineers; staff engineers should write in a personal style.
There is no such thing as over-applying idioms; more idiom is always better.

questions.explanation: The chapter's closing trade-off: over-applied idioms (clever reduce, maximal POP, hyper-granular types) can reduce clarity; optimize for the future maintainer, and sometimes the plain version wins. (b) misattributes the reason to performance rather than readability. (c) is false; the point is maintainability, not seniority signaling. (d) is exactly the reflex the trade-off warns against.
